docs.title: Lamia Documentation
docs.text: Lamia is a human-friendly scripting language with seamless Python integration and a comprehensive automation and validation framework that provides powerful tools for data validation, web automation, and LLM integration. It is designed for non-technical users.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
Get started with Lamia in just a few steps:

  1. Installation 
    pip install lamia-lang

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text:
• Getting Started: Installation and setup instructions
  • User Guide: .lm file syntax, web automation, validation
  • API Reference: Complete API documentation

docs.title: Community and Support
docs.text:
• GitHub: Repository
  • Issues: Bug Reports & Feature Requests

Ready to get started? Head over to the Installation Guide to begin using Lamia in your projects!

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
You don't need to know every detail of the configuration file. The only part that you need to know is the model_chain section where you configure the default and fallback models.

In a folder where you want to run Lamia, you can run lamia init. It will ask you a few questions and create a config.yaml file for you. 

lamia init

After running this command, Lamia will create a config.yaml file for you. Let's see what is inside.

docs.title: Configuration File Structure
docs.text:
Lamia uses YAML-based configuration files to customize behavior across all components. This guide covers the configuration system in detail.

A typical config.yaml file has the following structure:

# Core engine settings
engine:
  default_timeout: 30
  max_retries: 3
  log_level: "INFO"

# LLM provider configurations
model_chain:
  - name: "anthropic:claude-3-haiku-20240307" # primary model/provider
    max_retries: 2
#    temperature: 0.3          # cooler than provider default
#    max_tokens: 500
  - name: "openai"
    max_retries: 1
    temperature: 0.2

# Web automation settings
web_config:
  browser_engine: selenium          # Browser automation engine: selenium, playwright
  http_client: requests             # HTTP client library: requests
  browser_options:
    headless: false                 # Run browsers in headless mode
    timeout: 10.0                   # Default timeout in seconds for browser operations
  http_options:
    timeout: 30.0                   # Default timeout in seconds for HTTP requests
    user_agent: "Bot/1.0"           # User agent string for HTTP requests

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Lamia supports environment variable substitution using the ${VARIABLE_NAME} syntax:

docs.title: Common Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Description Required OPENAI_API_KEY OpenAI API key For OpenAI LLM usage ANTHROPIC_API_KEY Anthropic API key For Anthropic LLM usage

docs.title: Engine Configuration
docs.text:
engine:
  default_timeout: 30        # Default timeout in seconds
  max_retries: 3            # Maximum retry attempts
  log_level: "INFO"         # Logging level (DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR)
  parallel_execution: true  # Enable parallel step execution

docs.title: Anthropic Configuration:
docs.text:
The most important thing to take away from this for non-advanced LLM users is that you can set/change the default model with the default_model property. The default model is used when you don't specify a model, for example, when you specify 'anthropic' the 'anthropic:' will be selected. 

  # Anthropic Configuration
  anthropic:
    enabled: true
    default_model: claude-3-opus-20240229
    models:
      - name: claude-3-opus-20240229
        temperature: 0.2           # Make Claude more factual
        max_tokens: 4000
      - claude-3-sonnet-20240229   # Uses provider defaults
      - name: claude-3-haiku-20240307
        temperature: 0.4
      - claude-2.1
      - claude-2.0
    temperature: 0.7     # Higher = more creative, Lower = more focused
    max_tokens: 1000    # Maximum length of response
    top_k: 50          # Limit vocabulary for sampling
    top_p: 1.0         # Alternative to temperature for sampling

docs.title: Configuration Profiles
docs.text: You can use different configuration profiles for different environments:

docs.title: Usage with Profiles
docs.text:
# Development
lamia run workflow.lm --config config-dev.yaml

# Production  
lamia run workflow.lm --config config-prod.yaml

docs.title: Configuration Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use Environment Variables: Keep sensitive data in environment variables
  2. Reasonable Defaults: Provide sensible defaults for all optional settings

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This guide will help you install Lamia and set up your development environment.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10 or higher
  • pip (Python package installer)

docs.title: Using pip (Recommended)
docs.text:
Install Lamia directly from PyPI:

pip install lamia-lang

docs.title: Development Installation
docs.text:
For development or to get the latest features, install from source:

git clone https://github.com/lamia-lang/lamia.git
cd lamia
pip install -e .

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Verify your installation by running:

lamia -h

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
After installation, create a basic configuration file with

lamia init

or copy an existing config.yaml file to your project directory.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
lamia init will ask you to install the LLM API keys. But you can set up your environment variables with the following command:

export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-openai-api-key"
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your-anthropic-api-key"

Or create a .env file in your project directory:

OPENAI_API_KEY=your-openai-api-key
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=your-anthropic-api-key

docs.title: Import Error
docs.text:
If you encounter import errors, ensure you have the correct Python version: 

python --version  # Should be 3.10+

docs.title: Permission Error
docs.text:
On some systems, you might need to use sudo or install in a virtual environment: 

python -m venv lamia-env
source lamia-env/bin/activate  # On Windows: lamia-env\Scripts\activate
pip install lamia-lang

docs.title: Dependency Conflicts
docs.text:
If you have dependency conflicts, try creating a fresh virtual environment: 

python -m venv fresh-env
source fresh-env/bin/activate
pip install lamia-lang

For more help, check our GitHub Issues or create a new issue.

docs.title: Model Evaluation
docs.text: The evaluation module helps you find the most cost-effective model that meets your validation requirements. It tests models from most expensive to least expensive, finding the cheapest one that achieves your desired pass rate.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: lamia eval <your_script.lm>

docs.title: Search Strategies
docs.text:
Strategy Description binary_search (default) Efficiently finds cheapest working model step_back Tests from cheapest up to most expensive model

With both strategies, the eval extracts all LLM prompts from the script and evaluates them one by one. The evaluation stops on the first prompt where no model in the range succeeds. But if at least one model passes for a prompt, it moves on to the next prompt.

docs.title: Advanced usage through Python with pass rates
docs.text:
Rate Use Case 100.0% (default) Cheapest model that always works 90.0% Allow some failures, use with retry strategies 75.0% Creative tasks with more variation tolerance 
result = await evaluator.evaluate_prompt(
    prompt="My prompt",
    return_type=Markdown[MyRepresentationType], # MyType is a pydantic model
    max_model="openai:gpt-4o",
    required_pass_rate_percent=90.0
)

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution "No models available" Check API keys: OPENAI_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, or ensure Ollama is running "No pricing provider found" Normal — evaluation works without pricing data "Validation failed for all models" Simplify prompt, allow higher max_model, or check return_type, give more hints with the return_type, see the validation documentation on how to do this

docs.title: File Context for AI Prompts
docs.text: The files() context manager allows you to reference files in your LLM prompts using the {@filename} syntax. This is perfect for AI-powered form filling, document analysis, and any task where the AI needs access to local files.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
with files("~/Documents/", "~/projects/"):
    def answer_question(question: str, models="openai:gpt-4"):
        """
        Answer: {question}

        Use information from {@resume.pdf} and {@cover_letter.txt}
        """

docs.title: 🔍 Smart File Search
docs.text:
Files are resolved using multiple strategies (similar to Cursor):

  1. Exact filename match (highest priority)
  2. Content grep (searches inside files)
  3. Fuzzy matching (handles typos)
  4. Path component matching

docs.title: 📄 Automatic File Extraction
docs.text:
• PDF files: Text extracted using PyPDF2
  • DOCX files: Text extracted using python-docx
  • Text files: Read directly

docs.title: ⚠️ Error Handling
docs.text:
• AmbiguousFileError: Multiple files match with similar scores
  • FileReferenceError: File not found (with suggestions)

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
# linkedin_automation.lm

with files("~/Documents/"):
    def extract_name(models="openai:gpt-4"):
        """Extract my full name from {@resume.pdf}"""

    def answer_experience_question(question: str, models="openai:gpt-4"):
        """
        Answer this job application question: {question}

        Use {@resume.pdf} and {@cover_letter.txt} for context.
        """

# Call the functions
name = extract_name()
answer = answer_experience_question("What are your main skills?")

docs.title: Multiple Directories
docs.text:
with files("~/Documents/", "~/projects/linkedin/", "./config/"):
    # All files from these directories are indexed
    def setup(models="openai:gpt-4"):
        """Load API keys from {@credentials.json}"""

docs.title: Nested Contexts
docs.text:
with files("~/Documents/"):
    with session("linkedin", "https://linkedin.com/feed"):
        # Both files and session contexts are active
        def apply_to_job(models="openai:gpt-4"):
            """
            Fill the Easy Apply form using {@resume.pdf}
            """

docs.title: Exact Path
docs.text:
{@resume.pdf}                    # Searches for resume.pdf in indexed directories
{@path/to/resume.pdf}            # Relative path from indexed directory
{@/Users/me/Documents/resume.pdf} # Absolute path (always works)

docs.title: Fuzzy Matching
docs.text:
The search is typo-tolerant:

{@resum.pdf}      # Finds resume.pdf (fuzzy match)
{@my resume}      # Finds john_doe_resume.pdf (word matching)
{@config}         # Finds config.yaml, config.json, etc.

docs.title: 1. Filename Match (Score: +100)
docs.text:
with files("~/Documents/"):
    # Exact: resume.pdf (score: 150)
    # Prefix: resume_2024.pdf (score: 130)
    # Contains: my_resume.pdf (score: 100)
    {@resume}

docs.title: 2. Content Grep (Score: +50)
docs.text:
with files("~/projects/"):
    # Searches file contents for "database"
    # Useful for finding files by keywords
    {@database}

docs.title: 3. Fuzzy Match (Score: +30)
docs.text:
with files("~/Documents/"):
    # Handles typos and partial names
    {@resum}  # → resume.pdf
    {@cv}     # → john_doe_cv.pdf

docs.title: 4. Path Component Match (Score: +20)
docs.text:
with files("~/projects/"):
    # Matches directory names in path
    {@linkedin}  # → projects/linkedin/config.yaml

docs.title: AmbiguousFileError
docs.text:
Raised when multiple files have similar match scores:

from lamia import AmbiguousFileError

with files("~/Documents/"):
    try:
        def load_config(models="openai:gpt-4"):
            "Load {@config}"  # Multiple config.* files exist
    except AmbiguousFileError as e:
        print(f"Multiple files match '{e.query}':")
        for path, score in e.matches:
            print(f"  - {path} (score: {score:.2f})")

Solution: Be more specific: 

def load_config(models="openai:gpt-4"):
    "Load {@config.yaml}"  # Specific extension

docs.title: FileReferenceError
docs.text:
Raised when file cannot be found:

from lamia import FileReferenceError

with files("~/Documents/"):
    try:
        def load_data(models="openai:gpt-4"):
            "Load {@nonexistent.pdf}"
    except FileReferenceError as e:
        print(f"File '{e.query}' not found")
        print(f"Did you mean: {e.suggestions}")

docs.title: PDF Files
docs.text:
Requires PyPDF2: 

pip install PyPDF2


with files("~/Documents/"):
    def extract_info(models="openai:gpt-4"):
        """
        Extract name, email, and skills from {@resume.pdf}
        """

Output format: 

--- resume.pdf ---
--- Page 1 ---
John Doe
Senior Python Developer
...
--- Page 2 ---
...

docs.title: DOCX Files
docs.text:
Requires python-docx: 

pip install python-docx


with files("~/Documents/"):
    def summarize(models="openai:gpt-4"):
        "Summarize {@report.docx}"

docs.title: Text Files
docs.text:
No dependencies required:

with files("~/projects/"):
    def analyze(models="openai:gpt-4"):
        "Analyze code in {@main.py}"

docs.title: Multiple File References
docs.text:
def compare_documents(models="openai:gpt-4"):
    """
    Compare {@resume_v1.pdf} and {@resume_v2.pdf}

    List the differences between these two resumes.
    """

docs.title: Conditional File Loading
docs.text:
import os

resume_path = "~/Documents/resume.pdf" if os.path.exists("~/Documents/resume.pdf") else "./fallback_resume.pdf"

with files(resume_path):
    def get_name(models="openai:gpt-4"):
        "Extract name from {@resume.pdf}"

docs.title: Mixed Contexts
docs.text:
with files("~/Documents/"):
    # Variable substitution + file references
    def answer_question(question: str, company: str, models="openai:gpt-4"):
        """
        Answer this {company} job application question: {question}

        Use {@resume.pdf} for background information.
        """

docs.title: Ambiguity Threshold
docs.text:
Adjust how strict ambiguity detection is:

from lamia.engine.managers.files_context_manager import FilesContext

with FilesContext("~/Documents/") as ctx:
    ctx.AMBIGUITY_THRESHOLD = 20.0  # Default: 10.0
    # Higher = less likely to trigger AmbiguousFileError

docs.title: Search Threshold
docs.text:
Adjust fuzzy matching sensitivity:

from lamia.engine.managers.files_context_manager import FileSearcher

searcher = FileSearcher(files)
results = searcher.search("query", threshold=0.7)  # Default: 0.6
# Higher threshold = stricter matching

docs.title: Indexing
docs.text:
Files are indexed once when entering the context:

with files("~/Documents/"):  # ← Indexing happens here
    # Fast lookups inside the context
    result1 = func1()
    result2 = func2()

Best practices: - Use specific directories, not entire home folder - Avoid indexing node_modules/, .venv/, etc. (automatically skipped)

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
File contents are cached during a single context:

with files("~/Documents/"):
    # resume.pdf read from disk
    result1 = extract_name()

    # resume.pdf from cache (fast)
    result2 = extract_skills()

docs.title: Git-Aware Filtering
docs.text: The context automatically skips: - Hidden directories (.git/, .venv/) - node_modules/, __pycache__/ - Files matching .gitignore patterns

docs.title: Comparison with Cursor
docs.text:
Feature Cursor Lamia files() Syntax @filename {@filename} Exact path ✅ ✅ Fuzzy search ✅ ✅ Content grep ✅ ✅ Typo tolerance ✅ ✅ PDF extraction ❌ ✅ DOCX extraction ❌ ✅ Error suggestions ✅ ✅

Why {@} instead of bare @? - Avoids conflicts with @mentions, @everyone, email addresses - Consistent with {variable} syntax - Explicit and unambiguous

docs.title: Real-World Example: LinkedIn Easy Apply
docs.text:
# linkedin_automation.lm

with files("~/Documents/"):
    with session("linkedin", "https://linkedin.com/feed"):

        def navigate_to_jobs():
            web.navigate("https://linkedin.com/jobs")
            web.click("button[aria-label='Easy Apply']")

        def fill_application_form(models="openai:gpt-4"):
            """
            Analyze the current job application form and fill it.

            Use {@resume.pdf} for my background.
            Use {@cover_letter_template.txt} for writing style.

            Return JSON with field selectors and values:
            {
              "fields": [
                {"selector": "input[name='years_experience']", "value": "5"},
                {"selector": "textarea[name='why_interested']", "value": "..."}
              ]
            }
            """

        def submit_application():
            navigate_to_jobs()

            # AI analyzes form and generates answers
            form_data = fill_application_form()

            # Fill fields
            for field in form_data['fields']:
                web.type_text(field['selector'], field['value'])

            # Upload resume
            web.upload_file("input[type='file']", "~/Documents/resume.pdf")

            # Submit
            web.click("button[type='submit']")

        # Execute
        submit_application()

docs.title: "FileReferenceError: File 'resume.pdf' not found"
docs.text:
Cause: File not in indexed directories

Solution: 1. Check file exists: ls ~/Documents/resume.pdf 2. Add directory to context: with files("~/Documents/"): 3. Use absolute path: {@/full/path/to/resume.pdf}

docs.title: "AmbiguousFileError: Multiple files match 'config'"
docs.text:
Cause: Multiple files have similar scores

Solution: - Be more specific: {@config.yaml} instead of {@config} - Use path: {@linkedin/config.yaml}

docs.title: "PyPDF2 not installed"
docs.text:
Cause: PDF extraction requires PyPDF2

Solution: 

pip install PyPDF2

docs.title: Slow indexing
docs.text:
Cause: Indexing too many files

Solution: - Use specific directories, not ~ - Files are cached, so subsequent lookups are fast

docs.title: files(*paths: str) -> FilesContext
docs.text:
Create a files context manager.

Parameters: - *paths: File or directory paths to index

Returns: FilesContext manager

Example: 

with files("~/Documents/", "./config/"):
    # Use files here

docs.title: FilesContext.resolve_file_reference(query: str) -> str
docs.text:
Resolve a file reference to absolute path.

Parameters: - query: Filename or pattern

Returns: Absolute path to resolved file

Raises: - FileReferenceError: File not found - AmbiguousFileError: Multiple matches

docs.title: FilesContext.inject_file_references(prompt: str) -> str
docs.text:
Replace {@filename} references with file content.

Parameters: - prompt: Prompt string with {@} references

Returns: Prompt with injected file contents

docs.title: Required
docs.text: None (uses Python stdlib)

docs.title: Optional
docs.text:
• PyPDF2: PDF extraction
  • python-docx: DOCX extraction
pip install PyPDF2 python-docx

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Web Automation
  • Validation

docs.title: .hu File Syntax
docs.text:
.hu (human) files are plain-text prompt templates. They contain no Python -- just the text you want to send to an LLM, with optional parameter placeholders and file-context references.

Each .hu file is automatically a callable function whose name matches the filename. You call .hu functions from .lm files -- .lm files are the orchestration layer that ties everything together.

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
summarize.hu 

Summarize the following article: {@article.txt}
Focus on {aspect} and keep it under {max_words} words.

pipeline.lm 

result = summarize(aspect="key findings", max_words=200) -> HTML
print(result)

Run with: 

lamia pipeline.lm

docs.title: Parameter placeholders
docs.text:
Use {name} to define parameters. They are auto-detected -- no declaration needed.

Write a {tone} email to {recipient} about {topic}.

When called, all parameters must be passed as keyword arguments:

write_email = draft_email(tone="formal", recipient="the team", topic="Q3 results")

Missing parameters raise an error: 

draft_email() missing required keyword arguments: recipient, tone, topic

To include a literal brace in the text, double it: 

The result is in the format {{key: value}}.

docs.title: File context references
docs.text:
Use {@filename} to inject the contents of a local file into the prompt. This uses the same file-context resolution as .lm files -- fuzzy matching, absolute paths, and with files() scoping all work.

Review the code in {@main.py} and suggest improvements.
Also check {@utils.py} for any issues.

See the File Context documentation for details on search strategies and error handling.

docs.title: Return types
docs.text:
.hu files are return-type agnostic. The return type is specified by the caller in the .lm file using the -> syntax:

# Returns validated HTML
page = generate_landing_page(product="Lamia") -> HTML

# Returns a Pydantic model extracted from HTML
quote = get_stock_data(ticker="AAPL") -> HTML[StockQuote]

# Writes output to a file
generate_docs(project="lamia") -> File(Markdown, "docs.md")

If called without a return type, the raw LLM response text is returned.

docs.title: Calling conventions
docs.text:
• .hu functions are called from .lm files like regular functions
  • All parameters must be keyword arguments
  • .hu files cannot call other .hu files -- use .lm files as the glue
  • Filename (without .hu extension) must be unique across the project

docs.title: Standalone execution
docs.text:
You can run a .hu file directly from the CLI:

lamia hello.hu

This sends the prompt (with no parameters) to the default model and prints the response. Parameterized .hu files require a .lm wrapper to pass arguments.

docs.title: Comparison with .lm files
docs.text: Feature .hu files .lm files Content Plain text prompt Python + Lamia syntax Parameters {name} placeholders Python function arguments Return type Set by caller Declared with -> Type Python code Not supported Full Python support Imports Not needed Implicit Lamia imports Can call .hu No Yes Can call .lm No Yes Filename = function Yes No (uses def names)

docs.title: .lm File Syntax
docs.text:
Lamia .lm files use a hybrid Python syntax that lets you write LLM commands, file operations, and web actions as regular function definitions. Lamia syntax can be combined with normal Python code. So you get the power of Python combined with human-friendly syntax for LLM commands and other operations needed for daily tasks by non-technical users. You can run Lamia scripts with:

lamia your_script.lm

docs.title: The execution flow
docs.text:
Lamia detects its own syntax and executes through its engine, the rest of the code is executed as normal Python code. Here are some function definitions that will be executed by Lamia:

# LLM command — plain text goes to the AI
def generate_story():
    "Write a short story about a robot discovering emotions"

# File read — paths are read from disk
def read_config() -> JSON:
    "./config.json"

# Web request — URLs trigger HTTP requests
def fetch_data() -> JSON:
    "https://api.example.com/data"

Users must be careful, because unlike Python, Lamia functions must skip return statements. See the example below:

# The return statement makes this a Python function which returns the string "Write a short story about a robot discovering emotions"
def generate_story():
   return "Write a short story about a robot discovering emotions"

# This function is a Lamia function which sends the string "Write a short story about a robot discovering emotions" to the AI.
def generate_story():
   "Write a short story about a robot discovering emotions"

Lamia uses -> syntax to return data. In Python -> is used to define the return type of the function. In Lamia it is used to actually return a value or stream data.

docs.title: Return Types
docs.text:
Without return types, Lamia feels incomplete. Return type markers are more than markers, they are extractors and validators at runtime. Here is an example of how to use return types:

def get_data() -> JSON:
    "./users.json"   # Must be valid JSON - otherwise the function will fail

def generate_page() -> HTML:
    "Create a login form"   # Must be valid HTML - otherwise the function will fail

def plain_text() -> TEXT:
    "Write a summary"   # No validation, just extraction

Available types: HTML, JSON, YAML, XML, Markdown, CSV, TEXT

docs.title: Simplified Syntax
docs.text:
For one-line functions, defining functions seems like overkill sometimes. That is why Lamia provides a simplified syntax. The functions we wrote above can be shortened to:

"./users.json" -> JSON:

"Create a login form" -> HTML:

"Write a summary" -> TEXT:

But for these executions we won't be able to catch the result of the function. We need to assign the result to a variable.

data = "./users.json" -> JSON:

page = "Create a login form" -> HTML:

summary = "Write a summary" -> TEXT:

But if we are not interested in catching the values and we want to save the execution result to a file, we can use the -> File(...) syntax.

"./users.json" -> File(JSON, "users.json"):

"Create a login form" -> File(HTML, "login.html"):

"Write a summary" -> File(TEXT, "summary.txt"):

docs.title: Model Selection
docs.text:
Instead of simplified syntax, we need function definitions if we want to execute the function later, execute many times in different parts of the script, etc.

There is however a very important reason to use function definitions: model selection. All LLM commands in Lamia scripts use the global model chain coming from the config.yaml file mostly. But for some LLM commands users might need to use different models and even model chains. Here are examples of how to use model selection:

def analyze(models="openai:gpt-4"):
    "Analyze the data"

def with_fallback(models=["openai:gpt-4", "anthropic:sonnet-4"]):
    "Generate a report with model fallback"

# Same as above but gpt-4 will be retried twice before falling back to sonnet-4
def with_fallback(models=["openai:gpt-4", 'openai:gpt-4', "anthropic:sonnet-4"]):
    "Generate a report with model fallback"

docs.title: Variable substitution
docs.text:
Variable substitution is a feature that allows you to use variables in your LLM prompts, web actions and file operations. Example:

def stock_data(ticker: str = "AAPL") -> File(CSV[StockQuote], "stocks.csv", append=True):
    "extract the stock quote data https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/{ticker}"

for ticker in ["AAPL", "NVDA", "GOOG"]:
    stock_data(ticker)

# Or with simpler syntax
for ticker in ["AAPL", "NVDA", "GOOG"]:
    "extract the stock quote data https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/{ticker}" -> File(CSV[StockQuote], "stocks.csv", append=True)

docs.title: Reading Files
docs.text:
Use relative or absolute paths:

def read_data():
    "./data/input.txt"

def load_settings() -> JSON:
    "../config/settings.json"

def read_logs():
    "/var/log/application.log"

In addition, Lamia's file action API can be used:

content = file.append("/path/to/file.txt")
content = file.read("data.csv", encoding="latin-1")

docs.title: Writing to Files
docs.text:
As we have seen, the -> File(...) syntax can be used to save the execution result to a file. For complex file operations:

# With type validation
def generate_page() -> File(HTML, "output.html"):
    "Generate an HTML page about cats"

# Without type validation
def generate_text() -> File("output.txt"):
    "Generate some text"

# Append instead of overwrite
def add_rows() -> File(CSV, "data.csv", append=True):
    "Generate additional CSV rows"

# Custom encoding
def generate_latin() -> File("output.txt", encoding="latin-1"):
    "Generate text with special characters"

docs.title: File Context (with files())
docs.text:
Reference local files in LLM prompts using {@filename} syntax:

with files("~/Documents/"):
    def answer_question(question: str, models="openai:gpt-4"):
        """
        Answer: {question}

        Use information from {@resume.pdf} and {@cover_letter.txt}
        """

Files are resolved using smart search (exact match, content grep, fuzzy matching). PDF and DOCX are automatically extracted to text.

{@resume.pdf}                     # Search indexed directories
{@/Users/me/Documents/resume.pdf} # Absolute path
{@resum.pdf}                      # Fuzzy match → resume.pdf
{@config}                         # Finds config.yaml, config.json, etc.

Multiple directories and nested contexts:

with files("~/Documents/", "~/projects/"):
    with session("job_application"):
        def fill_form(models="openai:gpt-4"):
            "Fill the form using {@resume.pdf}"

For full details see the File Context Reference.

docs.title: Imports
docs.text:
Lamia avoids explicit imports. All Lamia types and interfaces are available without any import statements. But if you mix it with Python code, you will import Python dependencies as usual.

Because of implicit imports, IDEs will show warnings on some Lamia commands. They need to be ignored if the syntax is correct.

docs.title: Session Management
docs.text:
Use with session() for multi-step workflows that persist across runs:

def login_to_site():
    with session("login"):
        web.navigate("https://example.com/login")
        web.type_text("#username", "your_username")
        web.type_text("#password", "your_password")
        web.click("#login-button")

    # Always runs — session block is skipped if already completed
    web.navigate("https://example.com/dashboard")

First run: executes the block, saves session on success. Subsequent runs: detects valid session, skips the block.

Sessions are stored in .lamia_sessions/. Clear with rm -rf .lamia_sessions/.

docs.title: Web Actions
docs.text:
Use the web object for browser automation:

web.navigate("https://example.com")
web.click("#login-button")
web.type_text("#username", "user@example.com")
text = web.get_text(".result")
web.wait_for(".loading", "hidden")
web.screenshot("page.png")

Scoped elements:

modal = web.get_element("div.modal")
modal.click("button.close")

fields = web.get_elements("div.form-field")
for field in fields:
    label = field.get_text("label")
    field.type_text("input", "answer")

docs.title: Parameter Substitution
docs.text:
def generate_report(data: dict, style: str):
    "Create a {style} report based on: {data}"

docs.title: Advanced: Async and Parallel Execution
docs.text:
Functions can be defined as async and executed in parallel:

import asyncio

async def main():
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        get_weather(),
        read_config(),
        fetch_webpage()
    )

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Lamia validates LLM/Web/File responses automatically when you specify return types. Validators ensure outputs meet format and quality requirements before your code uses them.

docs.title: File Format Validators
docs.text: Type Description HTML Well-formed HTML JSON Valid JSON YAML Valid YAML XML Valid XML Markdown Valid Markdown CSV Valid CSV

docs.title: File Structure Validators
docs.text:
Validate file format AND match a Pydantic model schema:

class UserProfile(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int
    email: str

# In .lm files — validates JSON structure matches the model
def get_user() -> JSON[UserProfile]:
    "Generate a user profile"

Lamia's validation can be used in python code as well with:

result = lamia.run("Generate a user profile", JSON[UserProfile])

Available structure validators: JSON[Model], YAML[Model], XML[Model], HTML[Model], Markdown[Model], CSV[Model]

docs.title: Strict vs Permissive Mode
docs.text:
Each validator supports a strict flag (default: true):

  • strict: true: Only accepts pure, valid output (no extra text around it)
  • strict: false: Accepts output that contains a valid block within a longer response

When you want to be explicit about validation mode you can define file structure validators with JSON[Model, True/False], YAML[Model, True/False], XML[Model, True/False], HTML[Model, True/False], Markdown[Model, True/False], CSV[Model, True/False]

Here is what strictness does for each case:

Syntax Model Strict What happens HTML None True Plain HTML validation (well-formedness only), strict mode HTML[MyModel] MyModel False Structure validation against model, non-strict (default!) HTML[MyModel, True] MyModel True Structure validation against model, strict mode. Strict nesting is enforced HTML[MyModel, False] MyModel False Structure validation against model, explicitly non-strict. Any level of nesting

docs.title: File Structure Validation
docs.text: We will consider examples with HTML files, but the same principles apply to other file types.

docs.title: HTML Structure Validation for HTML generation
docs.text:
The following Lamia code will generate an HTML file with the structure defined in the HtmlStructure model and save it to the output.html file.

class Body(BaseModel):
    h1: str
    p: str
    p: str

class HtmlStructure(BaseModel):
    title: str
    body: Body

"Generate a blog post about the benefits of using AI" -> File(HTML[HtmlStructure, True], "output.html") # The True in HTML[HtmlStructure, True] flag means no additional nestings are allowed, the document should have the same nestings as defined in the model

When tried with Claude Sonnet 4, the output was 

<html>
<title>The Transformative Benefits of Using AI in Modern Life</title>
<body>
  <h1>The Transformative Benefits of Using AI in Modern Life</h1>
  <p>Artificial Intelligence has revolutionized how we work, learn, and solve problems in today's digital age. From automating repetitive tasks to providing intelligent insights from vast amounts of data, AI empowers individuals and organizations to achieve more with less effort. It enhances decision-making through predictive analytics, personalizes user experiences across platforms, and accelerates innovation in fields ranging from healthcare to education. By augmenting human capabilities rather than replacing them, AI serves as a powerful tool that frees us to focus on creative and strategic work while handling the mundane. As AI technology continues to evolve, its accessibility and applications expand, making it an indispensable asset for anyone looking to stay competitive and efficient in an increasingly complex world.</p>
</body>
</html>

This example script shows how you can use Lamia to generate HTML files with a desired structure all the time.

docs.title: HTML Structure Validation for HTML parsing
docs.text:
You can use HTML structure validation to parse HTML files to Pydantic models and use the model as you wish in your code. If the HTML structure is not as expected, the validation will fail and the rest of the code will not be executed. Validation makes sure that you extract the right data. When validation stops working, that might be a sign that the HTML structure has changed and you need to update the model.

Here is an example of simple web parsing with Lamia:

class Body(BaseModel):
    h1: str

class HtmlStructure(BaseModel):
    title: str
    body: Body

website_content = "https://example.com" -> HTML[HtmlStructure]
if website_content.body.h1 == website_content.title:
    print("Example.com website header is the same as the title: h1: {website_content.body.h1}, title: {website_content.title}")

If you open the page source of the https://example.com, you will see, at the time of writing this, the HTML structure is like this: ```html

Example.com Example.com ...

```

As you can see, as expected, the title is nested inside the tag. Additionally, the tag is not nested inside the tag. But the validation will succeed because the HTML structure is valid and we use HTML[HtmlStructure] in non-strict mode, which is why validation passes.

For parsing, usually the non-strict mode will be used. Otherwise, extensive Pydantic nested models might be needed to parse the HTML structure.

This is a useful, but simple example. Of course, in many real-world scenarios you will want to validate or extract complex HTML structures and using nested types to define them might be exhausting. That is why Lamia provides multiple additional ways to validate and extract data from the HTML structure. For example, you can use selectors to extract data from the HTML structure.

docs.title: Selector Usage with Validators
docs.text: For details on using selectors within file type validators, see the Selector Usage Guide.

docs.title: Web Automation
docs.text: Lamia provides browser automation and HTTP client operations for web-based tasks. You can use traditional CSS/XPath selectors or natural language descriptions powered by AI.

docs.title: Web Actions
docs.text:
Use the web object in .lm files:

# Navigation
web.navigate("https://example.com")

# Click, type, hover
web.click("#login-button")
web.type_text("#username", "user@example.com")
web.hover(".dropdown-menu")

# Get information
text = web.get_text(".result")
visible = web.is_visible(".modal")
enabled = web.is_enabled("button.submit")

# Wait and scroll
web.wait_for(".loading", "hidden")
web.scroll_to("#footer")

# Forms
web.select_option("#country", "US")
web.submit_form("#login-form")
web.upload_file("/path/to/resume.pdf", "input[type='file']")

# Screenshots
web.screenshot("page.png")

docs.title: Scoped Elements
docs.text:
Work within specific page elements:

# Single element
modal = web.get_element("div.modal")
modal.click("button.close")
modal.type_text("input", "search term")

# Multiple elements
fields = web.get_elements("div.form-field")
for field in fields:
    label = field.get_text("label")
    input_type = field.get_input_type()

    if input_type == InputType.TEXT:
        field.type_text("input", "answer")
    elif input_type == InputType.CHECKBOX:
        if not field.is_checked("input"):
            field.click("input")

# Get selectable options (radio, checkbox, dropdown)
options = field.get_options()  # Returns: ["Option A", "Option B", "Option C"]

docs.title: AI-Powered Selectors
docs.text:
Instead of CSS/XPath, describe elements in natural language:

# Natural language selectors
web.click("Sign in button")
web.type_text("Search input field", "lamia framework")
web.wait_for("Loading spinner", "hidden")

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Lamia detects the selector is natural language (not CSS/XPath)
  2. AI analyzes the page and returns the matching CSS selector

docs.title: Writing Good AI Selectors
docs.text:
# Good — clear and specific
"Sign in button"
"Search input field"
"Close modal button"
"Primary submit button"

# Bad — too vague
"button"
"click here"
"the thing"

docs.title: Ambiguity Handling
docs.text:
When multiple elements match, Lamia shows options:

AMBIGUOUS SELECTOR: 'Sign in'
Multiple elements match. Please be more specific:

Option 1: "Sign in"          → button.btn-primary
Option 2: "Sign in with Google" → button.google-signin
Option 3: "Sign in with Apple"  → button.apple-signin

docs.title: Mixing Selector Types
docs.text:
Use both in the same script:

web.click("button.submit")              # CSS selector
web.click("Sign in button")             # AI selector
web.type_text("//input[@name='email']", # XPath selector
              "user@example.com")
web.type_text("Email input field",      # AI selector
              "user@example.com")

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
In config.yaml:

web_config:
  browser_engine: selenium        # selenium or playwright
  http_client: requests           # HTTP client library
  browser_options:
    headless: true                # Run without visible window
    timeout: 10.0                 # Default timeout in seconds
  http_options:
    timeout: 30.0                 # HTTP request timeout
    user_agent: "Lamia/1.0"      # User agent string

docs.title: Browser Engines
docs.text: Engine Description selenium Traditional WebDriver-based automation (default) playwright Modern, fast browser automation

docs.title: Selector Cache
docs.text:
AI selector resolutions are cached to avoid repeated LLM calls:

.lamia_cache/selectors/selector_resolutions.json

# Clear cache if selectors stop working
rm -rf .lamia_cache/selectors/

docs.title: AI returns wrong selector
docs.text:
• Use more specific descriptions
  • Check if the page structure changed
  • Clear the selector cache and retry

docs.title: Ambiguous selector errors
docs.text:
• Add distinguishing details: "Primary sign in button" instead of "Sign in"
  • Use CSS selectors for elements that are easy to target

docs.title: Slow AI responses
docs.text:
• Use stronger/faster models in your model chain
  • Cached selectors are instant on repeat runs

docs.title: Selector Usage Guide
docs.text: This guide explains when and how to use json_schema_extra selectors with different file type validators.

docs.title: When to Use Selectors
docs.text: Use selectors when field names don't directly map to document elements:

docs.title: HTML Structure Validator
docs.text:
class UserProfile(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': '.profile-name'})
    email: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': '#user-email'})
    bio: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selectors': ['.bio-text', '.description', 'p']})  # Fallback chain

HTML Input: 

<div class="profile-name">John Doe</div>
<span id="user-email">john@example.com</span>
<p class="bio-text">Software developer</p>

docs.title: XML Structure Validator
docs.text:
class BookInfo(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': '//book/title'})
    author: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': '//book/author/@name'})
    pages: int = Field(json_schema_extra={'selectors': ['//pages', '//page-count']})  # Fallback

XML Input: 

<book>
    <title>Python Guide</title>
    <author name="Jane Smith" />
    <pages>250</pages>
</book>

docs.title: YAML Structure Validator
docs.text:
class Config(BaseModel):
    db_host: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': '$.database.host'})
    db_port: int = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': '$.database.port'})

YAML Input: 

database:
  host: localhost
  port: 5432

docs.title: CSV Structure Validator
docs.text:
class Employee(BaseModel):
    full_name: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': 'Full Name'})  # Column header
    employee_id: int = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': 'EmpID'})

CSV Input: 

Full Name,EmpID,Department
John Doe,12345,Engineering

docs.title: Markdown Structure Validator
docs.text:
class Article(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': 'h1'})
    summary: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': 'blockquote'})

docs.title: JSON Structure Validator
docs.text:
⚠️ WARNING: JSON field names map directly to keys. Use aliases instead of selectors.

# ❌ DON'T DO THIS (triggers warning)
class User(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': 'user_name'})  # Redundant!

# ✅ DO THIS INSTEAD
class User(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(alias='user_name')  # Use alias for different JSON keys

JSON Input: 

{
    "user_name": "John Doe",  // Maps to 'name' field via alias
    "age": 30
}

docs.title: Object Validator
docs.text: No selectors needed - ObjectValidator is for pure JSON → Pydantic validation without document parsing.

docs.title: HTML Selectors
docs.text:
• CSS Selectors: .class, #id, tag[attr="value"]
  • XPath: //div[@class="name"], /html/body/div[1]
  • Combination: ['#primary-name', '.backup-name', 'h1']

docs.title: XML Selectors
docs.text:
• XPath: //element, //element/@attribute, /root/child[1]
  • Fallback chains: ['//title', '//name', '//heading']

docs.title: YAML Selectors
docs.text:
• JSONPath: $.root.field, $.array[0].value
  • Dot notation: database.host, users[0].name

docs.title: CSV Selectors
docs.text:
• Column headers: "Full Name", "Employee ID"
  • Column indices: 0, 1, 2 (zero-based)

docs.title: Markdown Selectors
docs.text:
• Element types: h1, h2, blockquote, code
  • Position-based: h1[0], p[2]

docs.title: 🤖 AI-Powered Selectors
docs.text:
All validators support AI-powered selector resolution:

class SmartExtraction(BaseModel):
    # AI will try to fix invalid selectors or convert natural language
    title: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': 'find the main heading'})
    price: float = Field(json_schema_extra={'selectors': [
        '.price',           # Try CSS first
        '//span[@class="cost"]',  # Try XPath second  
        'extract the price'       # AI fallback
    ]})

Features: - Auto-correction: Fixes invalid CSS/XPath syntax - Natural language: Converts descriptions to selectors - No prefix needed: No ai: prefix required - automatic detection

docs.title: 1. Using Selectors with JSON
docs.text:
# Wrong - triggers warning
class JSONModel(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': 'user_name'})

# Correct - use alias
class JSONModel(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(alias='user_name')

docs.title: 2. Forgetting Fallbacks for Fragile Selectors
docs.text:
# Fragile - breaks if CSS changes
title: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selector': 'div.header-container > h1.main-title-text'})

# Robust - multiple fallbacks
title: str = Field(json_schema_extra={'selectors': [
    'div.header-container > h1.main-title-text',
    'h1.main-title-text', 
    'h1',
    '.title'
]})

docs.title: 3. Not Testing Selectors
docs.text: Always test your selectors with real data to ensure they work correctly!

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Use selectors: HTML, XML, YAML, CSV, Markdown validators
  • Don't use selectors: JSON validators (use aliases instead)
  • Provide fallbacks: For robust extraction
  • Test thoroughly: With real document samples
  • Leverage AI: For natural language and auto-correction
